title: I'll Be Gone in the Dark

chapters.title: The Golden State Killer
chapters.text:
Michelle McNamara gave the Golden State Killer his name. She wrote that he spread his crimes across California. He is also known as the East Area Rapist, the Early Bird Rapist, and the Original Night Stalker. He terrorized neighborhoods and committed around fifty rapes in Northern California and about twenty murders in Southern California.

For more than four decades, the killer stayed free and unnamed. His methods were almost ritual. He studied his victims' homes while they were away, learned the layout, looked at family photos, and slipped in through unlocked sliding windows. He even removed the bullets from guns he found.

While his victims slept, he woke them with a flashlight in their eyes. He spoke in a low, guttural whisper that survivors said sent a chill down the spine. His attacks looked planned and intelligent, and some pointed to possible military training.

Careful planning and a strong instinct for self-preservation marked the Golden State Killer's work.

During the years of his crimes, he was between eighteen and thirty, white, well built, and always masked.

He started with serial rape, stalking and attacking women in their beds. He later moved to surprising couples while they slept, binding them, and killing them. He sometimes left personal items behind, like pieces of jewelry or wall clocks.

The case stretched over a decade and pushed DNA analysis in criminal investigation forward. This summary follows how a clever criminal evaded capture for forty years and how his reckoning finally came.

As Michelle McNamara once wrote, most people carry a small Sherlock Holmes inside, waiting for the right set of clues.

Michelle McNamara

chapters.title: A tireless hunt for a serial rapist and killer
chapters.text:
Michelle McNamara poured time and resources into chasing the Golden State Killer's identity. Her pull toward unsolved murder cases began in Oak Park, Illinois, when she was fourteen years old.

Kathleen Lombardo, a twenty-four-year-old neighbor, was out jogging one evening with her Walkman. Her attacker dragged her into an alley and killed her by slitting her throat. Two days later, McNamara walked to the alley and picked up broken pieces of the Walkman from the ground. Right there, a strong curiosity about the killer took root, and unsolved murders became a lifelong obsession.

Police brushed off the story that witnesses saw a man step off a train and follow Lombardo. The rumor fed the idea that the killer came from somewhere else.

In her mid-thirties, McNamara turned that obsession into a website called True Crime Diary.

The True Crime Diary site set out to work both old and new cases.

Kathleen Lombardo's case went cold, but McNamara kept writing about it and brought it up in interviews. Twenty-six years after the killing, a childhood friend, Dan Olis, reached out. He had seen an interview where she mentioned the case. He told her that he and other boys had come across Lombardo's body. They believed they knew the killer, a man of Indian descent who walked out of the alley at the same time the body was found.

Kathleen Lombardo's killer was never caught.

chapters.title: Murders in Irvine and Dana Point, California
chapters.text:
David and Manuela Witthuhn made their home in Irvine, California. In February 1981, David was in the hospital with a bad stomach illness. Manuela kept a steady loop: dinner with her parents, a visit to David's hospital room, and the drive home. They shared phone calls around the clock.

On one of those days, Manuela never picked up. David rang her office and found out she had not come in at all. He called his mother-in-law, and Manuela was discovered on the bed, already gone.

Wrist marks told a clear story. Her hands and ankles had been bound. The killer had used a heavy lamp to crush her skull. A few pieces of jewelry and the tape from the answering machine had vanished, hinting that the killer did not want his voice on record. Some wondered whether Manuela had known the attacker, and eyes turned toward David and any possible lover.

Irvine police thought the case was strange, but criminalist Jim White of Orange County felt a flash of recognition as soon as he walked the scene.

A criminalist's job is to study a scene and pull out anything that can later be tested or compared.

White had seen many corners of the county in his years with the Orange County Sheriff's Department. Because his office ran the lab most local departments relied on, he could match patterns across city lines. He was certain the Witthuhn case shared fingerprints with the murder of Keith and Patty Harrington, a Dana Point couple killed half a year earlier.

Both scenes showed the same fingerprint. Victims were bludgeoned in their own beds, tied up, with ligature marks pressed into the skin. The women had been raped. The killer had grabbed the murder weapon and walked out. In both homes, wooden matches and tiny scorch marks, possibly from the killer, sat on the floor.

The likely connection was pushed aside, and both files went cold.

The early 1990s brought the first wave of usable DNA testing to the lab, and White's hunch returned. Working alongside another criminalist, Mary Hong, he pulled a thread between the two cases and, surprisingly, hooked an old third case, that of eighteen-year-old Janelle Cruz, whose body had been found in her family's Irvine home on May 5, 1986.

chapters.title: The East Area Rapist hits Sacramento
chapters.text:
From 1976 to 1977, the man later labeled the East Area Rapist, or EAR for short, hit Sacramento fourteen times.

His first known attack was in Rancho Cordova on June 18, 1976. The victim told police her attacker was muscular, wore gloves, and had no pants. He bound her wrists with cord, gagged her, and told her not to make a sound.

Detectives Richard Shelby and Carol Daly took over the case after the fifth assault. Three more attacks followed that month. By November 1976, the rapist had hit Sacramento County nine times in six months.

Survivors painted a steady picture: a white man in a mask, around five foot nine, somewhere in his twenties or thirties. He spoke in a low, angry whisper that climbed into a higher pitch when he was upset.

His rituals had a clear shape. He tied the hands of his victims and forced them to touch him. When he broke into a couple's home, he would take the woman into the living room and drape the TV to change the light. He never hurt young children directly, but the smaller ones were moved into another room and the older ones were tied up.

The Sheriff's Department first asked the local press to keep quiet so the publicity would not push the rapist out of town. Shelby, Daly, and a few others chased leads on their own. On October 18, when the rapist struck twice in twenty-four hours, the press blackout collapsed and the story broke wide open.

The same day, the rapist launched his ninth attack. He pointed a knife at a sixteen-year-old girl who had been watching TV alone, warned her not to move or she would be silent forever, and then slipped back into the dark.

Sacramento had at least five serial rapists operating in the 1970s, but the East Area Rapist was the one that stood out.

By May 1977, the East Area Rapist had struck Sacramento twenty times. Almost three thousand firearms were sold in the first five months of that year, and many couples took turns sleeping, with one partner watching the window with a rifle. The rapist later drifted from the east side of town to the south side.

Violent crime in the United States climbed through the 1960s and 1970s and reached its peak in the early 1980s.

chapters.title: How the Original Night Stalker grew careful
chapters.text:
In 1979, in Goleta, California, the man who would later be called the Original Night Stalker walked into a home and ordered a woman to tie up her boyfriend. He then bound her, led her to the living room, and started pacing. He cursed and chanted to himself that he would kill them both. In a flash of terror, the woman broke free through the front door while her boyfriend fled out the back. Their neighbor, an FBI agent, heard the scream and ran outside in time to spot a man riding away on a stolen bike. He gave chase, but the rider dropped the bike and vaulted the fence between two houses.

The couple described the Original Night Stalker to police, and that may have been the moment he decided no future victim would be left alive to talk.

The Original Night Stalker killed for the first time in December 1979.

That December, osteopathic surgeon Dr. Robert Offerman and his girlfriend, Alexandria Manning, were found dead. Manning lay on the bed with her wrists bound in white nylon twine, and Offerman was on his knees at the foot of the bed with his own hands bound in the same twine.

Some clues were left behind, including pry marks on a door, a star-shaped print from a pair of Adidas shoes, and a piece of nylon twine.

Neighbors reported break-ins and burglaries on the day of the killing. In the days after, investigators kept finding more bits of nylon twine in different spots around the neighborhood.

In 1981, the killer claimed another couple. Police counted twenty-four blunt wounds on the male victim, all matching the unknown tool used to kill the woman. This time, the killer took the bindings and the murder weapon with him, leaving almost nothing behind.

Investigators walked the neighborhood door to door, and a long list of couples said they had strange run-ins with the same man.

In 2000, in Orange County, Larry Pool noticed a link between the Goleta attacks and the Irvine cases. Years later, in 2011, DNA work finally tied the Goleta killings to the East Area Rapist.

chapters.title: Linking the Original Night Stalker to the East Area Rapist
chapters.text:
Working out of Contra Costa County, criminalist Paul Holes first came across the East Area Rapist case in 1997. He noticed that between October 1978 and July 1979, there were eleven EAR cases in the East Bay.

The Contra Costa County Sheriff's Office, or CCCSO, did not run the most advanced lab in California, but Holes still pieced together a working profile of the rapist. They concluded the same man who had been terrorizing Sacramento had resurfaced in the East Bay, right in Contra Costa County.

Holes had a starting DNA profile of the rapist. Better tools would later stretch that profile into something sharper.

He began pulling old files and dialing up victims and detectives who had already retired. One of those calls reached Larry Crompton, a retired detective on the CCCSO's EAR task force, who had taken the cases personally. Holes needed Crompton's memory of old suspects to see whether any of them could be tied to the EAR. Crompton pointed him at Santa Barbara first, because he had heard whispers about attacks that sounded like the EAR years before. The Santa Barbara County Sheriff's Department refused to cooperate.

Two decades had passed, and they still insisted they had no cases that matched his description. They redirected him to Irvine, where Holes met criminalist Mary Hong of the Orange County Sheriff's Department.

When Holes and Hong first compared notes, the CCCSO's lab was clearly behind the Orange County lab. They agreed to join forces. After a year and a half of shared work, the CCCSO's lab caught up, and DNA finally tied the East Area Rapist from Sacramento to the Original Night Stalker cases in the East Bay.

chapters.title: Building alliances from a shared obsession
chapters.text:
Michelle McNamara lit up around anyone who shared her need to unmask serial killers. In 2012, she began meeting investigators and detectives to gather what she could on the Golden State Killer, now referred to as the EAR-ONS, the East Area Rapist-Original Night Stalker.

She sat down with the social worker who acted as a gatekeeper between the Sacramento investigators and the online community, a message board crowd that traded evidence and theories about the EAR-ONS. She also met Paul Haynes, whom she nicknamed the Kid. He went on to become her research assistant and once spent close to four thousand hours combing through Ancestry.com and USSearch.com for likely subjects. He also pulled a copy of the 1997 Sacramento Suburban Directory and the 1983 Orange County telephone directory. He folded everything into a single list called the Master List, two thousand names long, with dates of birth, addresses, criminal records, and photos.

McNamara was relieved to find Haynes, who carried the same obsession. They both seemed a little embarrassed by how deep it ran.

In Sacramento, she met Richard Shelby, then set up an interview with Larry Pool in Orange County, who explained that eight thousand suspects had been checked over the years and stressed the role of DNA testing in clearing people.

She also met Paul Holes, who shared his working theories about the Golden State Killer and walked her through crime scenes in Contra Costa County, including San Ramon, Danville, Walnut Creek, and Davis.

chapters.title: The death of Michelle McNamara
chapters.text:
At just forty-six, Michelle McNamara died in her sleep on April 21, 2016. An undiagnosed heart condition was made worse by the pills she took for pain and anxiety. A week after her passing, Paul Haynes and Billy Jensen, an investigative journalist, gathered more than three thousand of her files on the Golden State Killer. They also had notebooks, police reports, and almost forty boxes of paperwork. At the time of her death, she was halfway through writing I'll Be Gone in the Dark, and Haynes and Jensen teamed up to finish and shape the material. Michelle wrestled with insomnia, and whenever she could not sleep, she turned to hunting for EAR suspects.

Michelle McNamara spent her life chasing the monster who had stolen ten lives and assaulted more than fifty women.

She had pulled together thousands of pieces of evidence from more than fifty-five crime scenes. On her hard drive, she tagged one document as Possibly Interesting People, a file stuffed with notes and details on possible suspects. On her blog, True Crime Diary, she left several questions open for readers, and they sent back clues and theories. She took every bit of help she could get.

As Michelle McNamara once wrote, a crime scene is time-sensitive and always on the edge of falling apart, and every person who walks in adds the risk of contamination.

Michelle McNamara

Investigators believed the Golden State Killer had lived or worked in Sacramento, even though his face and name were still unknown.

A year before her passing, Paul Haynes had begun using geographic profiling to narrow down areas near the killer's home.

Geographic profiling means studying locations tied to a string of crimes to map a serial offender's anchor points.

Most of the case pointed back to Sacramento, which made it the most important place to focus. In theory, finding the killer meant finding people who had lived in Sacramento through the late 1970s and then moved south to the Los Angeles area.

Geographic profiling and DNA databases were the strongest tools Paul Haynes and Billy Jensen had, but each came with limits. The two of them were not about to stop until a name was attached to the man.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Michelle McNamara spent her final years chasing the Golden State Killer. Sadly, she did not live long enough to see him caught.

As she got closer to solving the case, she still wanted justice rather than glory, and she was aware of the danger her obsession carried. She was so committed that she kept searching even as her health faded and sleep grew shorter.

Her husband, Patton Oswalt, said he was sure the killer did not have much time left before he was caught. Coincidentally, the police arrested Joseph James DeAngelo, age seventy-two, on April 25, 2018, near his home of thirty years in Citrus Heights.

DeAngelo, a former Auburn police officer, was linked by DNA evidence to the Golden State Killer and charged with twelve counts of homicide, fifty rapes, and the ransacking of more than one hundred twenty homes.

The arrest finally brought closure to victims, families, and detectives who had chased clues for years. Above all, it was a form of validation for Michelle McNamara, who had spent so much time and energy hunting for this criminal's identity.

Michelle McNamara's story shows that hard work and steady determination can leave a mark that outlives the person who carried it.

A small practice can carry these ideas forward. Do not be afraid to chase what sets the heart on fire. Be honest about personal obsessions, and do not hesitate to ask for help around them. Health, safety, and well-being should always come first.
